entry.title: How to Connect On-Premise ABAP System to SAP BTP (Business Application Studio)-BAS
entry.content:
SAP BTP (Business Application Studio):

In modern SAP architectures, customers build Fiori, RAP, and CAP applications on SAP Business Technology Platform (BTP) while keeping their ERP systems on-premise. To enable secure communication between SAP BTP and an on-premise ABAP system.

Prerequisites:

 1.SAP BTP Subaccount (Trial or Enterprise).

2.SAP Cloud Connector Installed.

3.On-Premises ABAP System Details.

Important:
Cloud Connector establishes an outbound encrypted connection. No inbound firewall ports are required.
Step-by-Step Configuration: Create Destination in BTP.
Step 1: Open SAP BTP Cockpit:  Open your browser and navigate to:  https://cockpit.hanatrial.ondemand.com/trial/#/home/trial   

This blog does not cover how to create a SAP BTP account, as many videos and tutorials and official SAP documentation already explain that process in detail.

Before proceeding, ensure:

  • You have access to an SAP BTP Global Account
  • A Subaccount is already created
  • Connectivity and Destination services are enabled
  • SAP Business Application Studio is subscribed

Once these prerequisites are completed, continue with the Cloud Connector configuration.

 Click on Go To Your Trail Account:

 After That Click On   Trail Button.

Click On Destination Button:

Click on Create Button:

 Select From Scratch Option and Provide  to   The Your On-Premises ABAP System   Detail:

Note: Username and Password  should be your On-Premise ABAP System

Example: 

Note:  While creating the Destination in SAP BTP, make sure the URL field is correctly.

  • Your Virtual Host name
  • The Port number
  • The correct protocol prefix
    Example: http://nayakhana.com:8899

The http:// prefix is mandatory.

After That Please click on Add Property  and Give to the Below Details .

Note:    Hear sap-client---> 100 means this  is  your On-Premise ABAP System Client Number.

Example:

Once you have maintained all required details of your On-Premise ABAP system in the Destination configuration:

  • URL (Virtual Host + Port)
  • Proxy Type = OnPremise
  • Authentication = BasicAuthentication
  • Backend Username
  • Backend Password
  • Additional Properties (sap-client, etc.)
  • Click on “Check Connection”.

If you have maintained all configuration details correctly in  SAP BTP Destination, and you click Check Connection, you may see the message:"Backend is reachable from the Cloud Connector".

This confirms that the first step of BTP Destination connectivity is completed successfully.

Please Go  to The  Below link For:

 Step 2-  SAP Cloud Connector.
https://community.sap.com/t5/abap-blog-posts/how-to-connect-sap-cloud-connector-to-sap-btp/ba-p/143… 
Step 3:   Connect BAS To  SAP On-Premise System:

https://community.sap.com/t5/abap-blog-posts/connect-sap-business-application-studio-bas-to-on-premise-abap-system/ba-p/14340482 



Thank You,

B Naresh Kumar Nayak.

entry.title: How Do I Activate a Situation Type in the Extended Framework?
entry.content:
Situation Handling notifies users about issues requiring their attention. You may choose SAP delivered situation templates or design your own use cases. For the extended framework for Situation Handling, we often discussed how you can create custom situation templates. The framework comes also with SAP delivered use cases that you can easily activate.

This blog post explains how you can activate situation types from SAP delivered situation templates with the extended framework for Situation Handling. It has been available since SAP S/4HANA Cloud 2202 and SAP S/4HANA 2021 FPS02.

 

SAP Delivered Use Cases

Looking for Situation Handling use cases? Visit SAP Help and navigate to Situation Handling, then choose Use Cases. You can narrow your results by filtering for Situation Template Type (either extended object-based or message-based), area, and topic. To learn more about a specific use case, simply check the link in the Situation Template column for a detailed description.

Use Case

For illustration purposes we use the situation template Expiration of Purchase Contracts (PROC_ABOUT_TO_EXPIRE_CTR). Purchasers get a heads-up for purchase contracts that are about to expire.

 

Activation Overview for 2SL and 3SL

Before discussing the activation steps in detail, let me point out the differences between system landscapes and their specific transport paths.

In the extended framework for Situation Handling, you create a situation type from a situation template. Situation templates are only visible in the quality system of a 2-system landscape (2SL) and the development system of a  3-system-landscape (3SL). The situation type needs to be transported to the production system. The transport paths differ between 2SL and 3SL.

Transport the Situation Type in 2SL

In a 2-system-landscape, you transport the situation type from the quality system to the production system.

  • In the quality system you
    • Create a situation type from a situation template
    • Test if the configuration alerts the right users about the situation
    • Export the well-defined situation type
  • In the production system you
    • Import the situation type
    • Since the data usually differs between quality and production system you need to enter the appropriate values for the condition filters
    • Enable the situation type

Transports in 2-System-Landscape

Transport the Situation Type in 3SL

In a 3-system-landscape, transports are required from the development system to both the test and production system. In a 3SL environment you may also have different tenants for development, customizing, and parallel customizing that need to be considered as well.

  • On the customizing tenant in the development system you
    • Create a situation type from a situation template
    • Export the situation type
  • On the test tenant in the test system you
    • Import the situation type
    • Since the data usually differs between systems you need to enter the appropriate values for the condition filters
    • Test if the configuration alerts the right users about the situation
    • Forward the well-defined situation type
  • On the production tenant in the production system you
    • Import the forwarded situation type
    • Enter the appropriate values for the condition filters
    • Enable the situation type

Transports in 3-System-Landscape

Detailed Activation of a Situation Type in 3SL

Now let’s check the detailed steps for a sample use case in a 3-system-landscape as it is more comprehensive. In 2SL the export / import mechanism resembles the one from development system to test system in 3SL.

Depending on your system setting, the transports may also be processed automatically. For optimal clarity, the blog post discusses the transport steps through manual triggers.

Development System

Create a Situation Type from a Situation Template

Log-in as business process configuration expert on the customizing tenant in the development system. Open the Manage Situation Types – Extended app where you see a list of SAP delivered templates. Use the search option to find the template for our use case Expiration of Purchase Contracts (PROC_ABOUT_TO_EXPIRE_CTR). Select the template and an object page opens. In the header, select Create Situation Type.

Create situation type from situation template

A new page opens with the situation type that contains all information from the situation template. You just need to enter an ID, Name and Description. The type is configured and ready to use. Depending on your requirements you could adapt the settings, for example, by adding filter values for the conditions, changing texts or rescheduling the batch job.

I recommend monitoring the instances to see how situations are handled.

Monitor situations

Let’s check the relevant settings and understand how the configuration works.

There are two Situation Triggers that detect situations and cover different usage scenarios. While the batch-based trigger checks for situations in the background, the event-based trigger reacts on every active change of the contract.

Situation triggers

The situation triggers cover the following cases:

  • The Event-Based Trigger checks the conditions whenever you create or update a purchase contract.
    • Condition 1 checks if the validity period of a purchase contract expires within the next 30 days and triggers a situation. The information displayed to the user is defined in Situation Display number 1.
    • Condition 2 is only executed if condition 1 did not match. That is, the validity period is not within the next 30 days, for instance when the contract got extended, and the situation is closed. As nobody will be informed about the closure, the texts are empty (Situation Display number 2).

Event-based trigger

  • In addition to the events, the Batch-Based Trigger regularly checks the validity end date according to the batch schedule.
    • Condition 1 checks if the validity period of a purchase contract expires within the next 30 days and triggers a situation.

Batch-based trigger

The configuration for the Batch Job Scheduling specifies that the contract dates are verified every 3 hours throughout the day, beginning at 6:00 a.m.

Batch job scheduling

Export the Situation Type

Log-in as administrator on the customizing tenant in the development system. Open the Export Software Collection app.

  • On top of the table choose Create Software Collection and a new page opens
  • Choose Add Items and select the situation type you created
  • On the header select Check and wait until the check is processed successfully
  • Then choose Export and wait until the export is finished successfully

Test System

Import the Situation Type

Log-in as administrator on the test tenant in the test system. Open the Import Collection app.

  • Choose your software collection and a new page opens
  • On the header select Manual Import and wait until the check is processed successfully
  • Then choose Import on the bottom and wait until the import is finished successfully

Test the Situation Type

As business process specialist you can test the situation type on the corresponding tenant.

Make sure that the situation type is enabled.

Activate the situation type

Then maintain the conditions. The filter values are not transported since they may differ in the various systems and tenants.

  • Select the Event-Based Trigger and a new page opens
    • Condition 1 select Edit and enter the filter values
      • Ext. Reference ID: enter Empty, using the value help
      • Validity Period End: enter Next30Days, using the value help
    • Condition 2 select Edit and enter the filter values
      • Ext. Reference ID: enter Empty, using the value help
    • Go back to the main page

Event-based trigger

  • Select the Batch-Based Trigger and a new page opens
    • Condition 1 select Edit and enter the filter values
      • Ext. Reference ID: enter Empty, using the value help
      • Validity Period End: enter Next30Days, using the value help
    • Go back to the main page

Batch-based trigger

With the situation triggers, you defined when a situation is detected. Now, you need to define who should be informed about situations. Maintain the team(s) in the Manage Teams and Responsibilities app and ensure that at least one team is enabled. The filter settings work the same way as with the standard framework for Situation Handling.

Situation recipients

Now you can test the situation type in your business area with the corresponding application data. You can check if there are purchase contracts that match the conditions by choosing Trigger Batch and Manage Instances.

Test the type with trigger batch

If no situations were detected you can log-in as purchaser and create or manipulate a purchasing contract. Add an end date within the next 30 days and the save event will trigger a situation.

When you log-in as a team member of the recipients team you can also check, if you were alerted about the situation.

After the successful test you push the situation type to the production system.

Forward the Situation Type

Log-in as administrator and open the Import Collection app.

  • Select your software collection and a new page opens
  • On the header select Forward and wait until the validation is processed successfully
  • Then choose Forward on the bottom and wait until the export is finished successfully

Production System

Import Collection to Production System

As administrator open the Import Collection app on the production tenant in the production system.

  • Choose your software collection and a new page opens
  • On the header select Manual Import and wait until the check is processed successfully
  • Then choose Import on the bottom and wait until the import is finished successfully

Activate the Situation Type

Log-in as business process specialist and open the Manage Situation Types – Extended app. Like in the test system, you need to maintain the situation type.

  • Enable the situation type
  • Add the filter values for the trigger conditions
  • Ensure that at least one recipient team is enabled

Now you are all set.

Further Information

You find many more blog posts in the Best Practices section of Intelligent Situation Handling in the SAP Community. And you can visit SAP Help Portal for further guidance on Situation Handling.

entry.title: Practical Lessons in the Development of Advanced RAG Architectures in SAP Business AI
entry.content:
The Challenge

RAG (Retrieval-Augmented Generation) has quickly become the backbone of modern enterprise AI. In the SAP ecosystem—where business processes rely on massive, complex, and varied documents—RAG isn't just a buzzword. It's a critical layer of contextual intelligence that makes corporate data actually usable.

When you get into real-world projects, you realize RAG is about much more than just building conversational chatbots. We’re talking about deep support assistants that can handle complex business workflows using unstructured data. Think along the lines of:

  • Assistants tackling highly technical documentation queries

  • Automated document validation in Procurement

  • Verification of mandatory contractual clauses

  • Intelligent support for financial and logistics operations

  • Contextual analysis of internal procedures and policies

In these scenarios, a "plausible" answer isn't good enough. The business needs precise, traceable, and reliable responses that meet strict enterprise standards. Moving from a flashy demo to a scalable, production-ready solution doesn't just depend on picking a good generative model. It comes down to two structural factors:

  • Ingestion quality (grounding): How we transform raw content into meaningful units that can be retrieved accurately.

  • Relevance of retrieved content (retrieval): The quality of the context we feed the LLM so it can generate the right answer.

This is exactly why a proof-of-concept might look great on day one, but fall apart in production. If your ingestion breaks document structure, or your retrieval misses the key details, the LLM is flying blind—leading to incomplete answers, confusion, and hallucinations. The real challenge isn't just technical; it's architectural and strategic.

 

There Is No Single “Winning” Architecture: The Toolbox Matters

A common trap when approaching RAG is searching for the one "perfect" combination of chunking, embeddings, vector stores, and LLMs. The reality? The right stack depends entirely on your use case.

Because of this, our team at AYESA didn't just try to guess the final architecture. Instead, we treated this as a rigorous process: comparing techniques side-by-side and measuring their real-world impact. The real value of this three-month exercise was figuring out:

  • Which technique actually adds value in a specific context

  • The hidden costs associated with each setup

  • The risks introduced by different methods (like loss of structure, added noise, or semantic degradation)


Starting Point: Quantitative Evaluation Framework

To cut through the noise and avoid subjective opinions ("this answer looks okay"), we set up a strict evaluation framework using RAGAS (https://docs.ragas.io). We built a control dataset of 11 highly complex technical questions alongside their ideal answers (Ground Truth).

We weighted our metrics to explicitly balance retrieval and generation, giving us a reliable final score:

  • Context Recall (40%): Does the retrieved context actually contain the necessary information? (This is critical).
  • Faithfulness (20%): Is the answer based strictly on the context, or is the model hallucinating
  • Answer Relevancy (20%): Does the answer actually address the user's intent?
  • Context Precision (20%): Does the most relevant information show up at the top of the search results?

To keep everything secure and governed, all generative interactions with the LLM were orchestrated through SAP AI Launchpad.

Evolution Sequence: From V1 to V7

During our research, we documented seven iterative versions. We defined each version by pairing a specific Grounding strategy (G) with a Retrieval strategy (R). You can see how our global score evolved here:

 

Here is a breakdown of our journey: the techniques, the tools, and why we got the results we did. Our base architecture relied on SAP AI Core, SAP AI Launchpad, and HANA Cloud.

 

V1 - Simple RAG (Baseline)

Technique:

  • Grounding (G1): fixed-size document chunking
  • Retrieval (R1): similarity search in SAP HANA Cloud, retrieving the top 3 chunks

Baseline score: 0.73

Value and Applicability: V1 is basically our minimum viable architecture. It’s great for getting a quick baseline and validating the end-to-end pipeline (ingestion, embeddings, vector store, retrieval, and generation).

Why It Falls Short: When dealing with complex documentation, fixed chunking is brutal—it breaks tables in half and separates closely tied concepts. Also, retrieving only 3 fragments limits your context recall, especially for questions that require the LLM to synthesize interconnected information.

                                                                                                                                                                                    

V2 - Parent/Child Strategy

Technique

  • Grounding (G2): Small “child” chunks for precise searching, mapped to larger “parent” chunks that preserve the full context.

  • Retrieval (R2): We search against the children, but pass the parents to the LLM for the final context.

Score: 0.78 👍

Value: This is a lifesaver when documents have strict hierarchies (sections, subsections, annexes). It boosts search precision without destroying the semantic flow.

Why It Improves: By retrieving the “parent,” the LLM isn't forced to guess based on isolated, out-of-context fragments. V2 gave us a much better balance between precision and having enough context to work with.

                                                                                                                                                                                    

V3 - LLM-Generated Chunks and Keywords

Technique

  • Grounding (G3): LLM-generated chunks and keywords
  • Retrieval (R3): similarity search + keyword-based reordering

Score: 0.60 👎👎 (worse than V2)

Why It Failed Here:
Using an LLM for chunking introduced unexpected risks:

  • The model created segments that didn't match the actual semantic boundaries of the text.

  • The generated keywords often failed to accurately represent the highly technical domain. In this case, trying to add more "intelligence" actually degraded the quality.

                                                                                                                                                                                    

V4 - Multi-Query + Reranker #1 (MS MARCO)

Technique

  • Grounding (G2):Back to the Parent/Child strategy.
  • Retrieval (R4): Generate 3 query variants and rerank the resulting chunks using MS MARCO.

Score: 0.66 👍

Why Improvement Was Limited: MS MARCO is a great general-purpose reranker, but that’s exactly the problem. In domains heavily loaded with SAP-specific terminology, it tends to reorder chunks based on generic semantics that misalign with the technical intent.

                                                                                                                                                                                    

V5 - Reranker #2 (BGE)

Technique

  • Grounding (G2): Parent/Child
  • Retrieval (R5): A broad search (Top 10) followed by specialized reranking using BGE, keeping the Top 3.

Score: 0.83 👍👍 (significant jump)

Why It Works: Casting a wider net during the initial retrieval dramatically improves Context Recall, while the specialized reranking fixes Context Precision. This one-two punch is highly effective for production-grade enterprise assistants.

                                                                                                                                                                                    

V6 - Architecture with Cohere

Technique

  • Grounding (G2): Parent/Child
  • Retrieval (R6): broad search (Top 10) + Cohere commercial reranker, keeping top 3.

Score: 0.84 👍 and Context Recall 95% 👍

Value: This setup maximizes coverage without overwhelming the LLM with useless context. Commercial rerankers like Cohere add a layer of robustness and generalization that is hard to beat.

                                                                                                                                                                                    

V7 -  Final Architecture (SAP HANA Native Cross-Encoder)

Technique

  • Grounding (G2): Parent/Child
  • Retrieval (R7): We swapped the third-party reranker for SAP’s native Cross-Encoder (December 2025).

Score: 0.87 👍  and Context Recall 100% 👍 👍 👍

Analysis: This was the game-changer. By using a reranker specifically trained and aligned with SAP’s enterprise domain, we maxed out our precision. Hitting 100% recall proved we had finally eliminated our retrieval blind spots.

 

Conclusions, Lessons Learned, and Best Practices

Ingestion Is Not a Detail: It Is Central Design

  • V1 → V2 confirms naive chunking is insufficient. Parent/Child acts as a quality safeguard.
  • Best practice: Preserve your hierarchies, tables, and dependencies. Do not just chunk text—chunk meaning.

Generalist Components May Fail in Technical Domains

  • V4 was a harsh reminder that generic rerankers won't necessarily beat strategies designed to respect domain specifics.
  • Best practice: Prioritize models aligned with your technical terminology, and measure everything with frameworks like RAGAS.

Winning Pattern: Broad Retrieval + Specialized Reranking

  • The progression from V5 to V7 proved this is the most reliable enterprise strategy.
  • Best practice: Investing time and resources into a good reranker is almost always more profitable than endlessly "prompt tuning" your LLM.

Explicit Metrics Prevent Subjective Debates

  • RAGAS gave us the objective truth. Weighting recall so heavily reflects a practical reality: if the model doesn't have the right context, it cannot give a safe answer.
  • Best practice: Define what "quality" means for your project upfront (faithfulness, recall, precision, relevance) and stick to it.

Alignment with the SAP Ecosystem

  • V7 proved that leaning into native SAP components not only simplifies the architecture but delivers superior performance.

Final Conclusion

This three-month deep dive by the SAP Business AI team at AYESA proves that excellence in enterprise RAG doesn't come from finding a "magic model." It comes from iterative architecture and ruthless, rigorous evaluation. Our evolution to V7 provides a solid blueprint for architects looking to deploy reliable, high-performance assistants on SAP #BTP.

 

@ChristophMorgen @mfath @mkemeter @thiago

entry.title: BM25 and Hybrid Search for RAG on SAP HANA Cloud (without PAL)
entry.content:
Introduction

Vector search is a common approach for RAG (Retrieval-Augmented Generation) pipelines, but if you have worked with it you'll see that it's power is also a weakness sometimes... Normally you will make use of this type or search to find content by meaning but, what if you want to search for an exact mach? 

When a user asks about "article 12" or a document code like "HR-NORM-2024/0003", semantic search returns vaguely related content instead of the exact document. You need keyword search too. SAP HANA Cloud doesn't have built-in BM25 ranking without PAL (Predictive Analysis Library), which isn't always available or licensed. This post introduces langchain-hana-retriever, an open-source package I built that brings BM25 keyword search and hybrid retrieval to HANA Cloud without requiring PAL.

What it does

langchain-hana-retriever provides two LangChain-compatible retrievers:

  • HANABm25Retriever -- keyword search with BM25 scoring
  • HANAHybridRetriever -- combines vector search with BM25, merged via Reciprocal Rank Fusion

Both implement LangChain's BaseRetriever interface. They drop into any existing LangChain or LangGraph pipeline.

 

How BM25 works on HANA without PAL

LIKE doesn't work on NCLOB columns in HANA. Since most RAG setups store chunked text in NCLOB, standard substring matching fails. The workaround is LOCATE with TO_NVARCHAR:

 

SELECT "VEC_TEXT", "SOURCE"
FROM "MY_DOCS"
WHERE LOCATE(TO_NVARCHAR("VEC_TEXT"), 'article') > 0
   OR LOCATE(TO_NVARCHAR("VEC_TEXT"), '12') > 0
LIMIT 50

 

This fetches candidates, rows containing any of the query tokens. Then the rank-bm25 Python library scores them with BM25Okapi (term frequency, inverse document frequency, document length normalization). The result is a properly ranked list, not just "contains the string."

Installation

 

pip install langchain-hana-retriever

 

For hybrid retrieval (adds langchain-community for HanaDB vector store):

 

pip install "langchain-hana-retriever[hybrid]"

 

BM25 keyword retriever

 

import hdbcli.dbapi
from langchain_hana_retriever import HANABm25Retriever

connection = hdbcli.dbapi.connect(
    address="your-host.hanacloud.ondemand.com",
    port=443,
    user="DBADMIN",
    password="your-password",
    encrypt=True,
)

retriever = HANABm25Retriever(
    connection=connection,
    table_name="YOUR_TABLE",
    content_column="VEC_TEXT",
    metadata_columns=["SOURCE", "PAGE_START"],
    k=10,
    candidate_limit=50,
)

docs = retriever.invoke("article 12 reporting requirements")
for doc in docs:
    print(doc.metadata["SOURCE"], "-", doc.page_content[:100])

 

candidate_limit controls how many rows HANA returns before BM25 re-ranks. Set it higher than k (default 50 vs 10). max_tokens_in_query limits query tokens in the SQL WHERE clause (default 5) to avoid sending too many OR conditions.

Hybrid retriever

Vector search finds semantically similar content. BM25 finds exact matches. Hybrid gives you both.

 

from langchain_community.vectorstores import HanaDB
from langchain_openai import OpenAIEmbeddings
from langchain_hana_retriever import HANABm25Retriever, HANAHybridRetriever

embeddings = OpenAIEmbeddings(model="text-embedding-3-small")

vector_store = HanaDB(
    connection=connection,
    embedding=embeddings,
    table_name="YOUR_TABLE",
)

keyword_retriever = HANABm25Retriever(
    connection=connection,
    table_name="YOUR_TABLE",
    k=10,
)

hybrid = HANAHybridRetriever(
    vector_store=vector_store,
    keyword_retriever=keyword_retriever,
    alpha=0.5,
    k=10,
)

docs = hybrid.invoke("eligibility requirements article 12")

 

alpha controls the balance: 1.0 = vector only, 0.0 = keyword only, 0.5 = equal weight. Results are merged with Reciprocal Rank Fusion (RRF, k=60).

When to use what

  • Vector only: conceptual queries ("what are the eligibility requirements for subsidies")
  • BM25 only: exact lookups ("article 12", "HR-LIQUI-2024/0003", specific amounts)
  • Hybrid: queries mixing concepts with specific references ("eligibility requirements under article 12")

Hybrid with alpha=0.5 is a safe default for most RAG use cases.

Technical notes

  • LOCATE(TO_NVARCHAR(column), token) is the HANA workaround for NCLOB substring matching. TO_NVARCHAR has a default length limit -- candidate retrieval checks a truncated version, BM25 scoring uses the full text.
  • BM25 corpus is built per query from candidates. Works well for RAG (small candidate sets) but not designed for full-table ranking of millions of rows.
  • Async methods delegate to sync because hdbcli is a synchronous C-extension driver.
  • Deduplication in hybrid mode matches by page_content to avoid returning the same chunk from both sources.

Conclusion

If you're doing RAG on HANA Cloud and want better retrieval quality than vector search alone, this package gives you BM25 keyword ranking and hybrid search without needing PAL. Install it with pip install langchain-hana-retriever and try it on your existing HANA tables.

The package is MIT licensed and I welcome contributions. If you also need persistent agent state in HANA, check out the companion package langgraph-checkpoint-hana.

  • PyPI: https://pypi.org/project/langchain-hana-retriever/
  • GitHub: https://github.com/stubborncoder/langchain-hana-retriever

entry.title: Don’t Rush Configuration Without Knowing the Purpose
entry.content:
When I first started learning SAP FICO, I was very excited about configuration. I wanted to go into SPRO, create G/L accounts, define document types, and try everything quickly. It felt like I was making real progress just by completing configuration steps.

But as I continued learning, I realized something important: configuration only truly makes sense when we understand the business purpose behind it.

In SAP, settings are not just technical tasks. They represent how a real company runs its financial processes. For example, a G/L account is not just a number in the system, it shows how a company tracks its money. A posting key is not just a code ,it controls how transactions affect accounts. When I didn’t fully understand these purposes, I found myself memorizing steps instead of really learning.

I also noticed that rushing into configuration without understanding the business side can easily lead to mistakes. Wrong settings can cause incorrect postings, reporting issues, and confusion later when trying to fix problems.

Now, before I look at configuration, I try to ask myself:

  • What business process is this configuration supporting?
  • What is the financial impact of this setting?

When I focus on the purpose first, configuration becomes much more logical and easier to remember. It feels less like following steps and more like thinking the way an SAP consultant should think.

I’m still learning every day, but this mindset has helped me a lot. Take your time, understand the business, and then configure with confidence. @SAPSAP4  #FICO

entry.title: Q&A section to have Public Cloud or Private Cloud option to choose
entry.content: Of late, almost all posts are being created without a clear distinction as to whether the requirement or issue is in Private Cloud or Public Cloud.  Since the solution varies depending upon this, why don't the concerned SCN Development team to set a mandatory option whoever creates a new discussion?  Of course the drill down option can be ECC / On-Premise / Private Cloud / Public Cloud

entry.title: The 63% Barrier: Is Your Strategy Outpacing Your Talent?
entry.content:
The world is moving through a period of rapid technological acceleration that is doing more than just reshaping tools, but rather changing the DNA of jobs, industries, and entire economies. According to the World Economic Forum (2025)1 , 86% of employers expect AI and information processing technologies (including Big Data, VR, AR, etc.) to transform their business.

However, this transition has unveiled a critical threat to business success. While technology is the primary engine of transformation, the Future of Jobs Report reveals that 63% of employers view the skills gap as the single biggest barrier to progress. Today, only 2 out of 10 business leaders believe the current education system effectively develops the necessary AI and data skills.

In this high-stakes environment, equipping talent with the right skillset is no longer just an HR matter, but a strategic asset for global competitiveness and societal progress.

Bridging the “Readiness Gap”

While technology is the engine of transformation, 63% of employers view the skills gap as the biggest barrier to progress. To address this, SAP has invested in capacity-building programs like the SAP Young Professionals to support the SAP Ecosystem (including Partners and Customers) in securing the talent they need to differentiate themselves in the digital era.

Many platforms offer beginner-level AI certifications in as little as 30 hours 2. However, these often lack the industry alignment that matters most. Therefore, the SAP Young Professionals is designed as an intensive enablement journey, led by world-class experts and aligned strictly with the ecosystem’s needs to drastically reduce "ramp-up" time for new hires.

It is not enough to simply “know AI”. Success requires understanding the specific needs of Customers, who represent 99 of the 100 largest companies in the world. Consequently, the program focuses on the high-demand Core Skills for 2030 identified by the WEF:

  • Artificial Intelligence
  • Technological literacy (Encompassing digital skills and programming)
  • Soft skills such as: analytical thinking, leadership, resilience, agility, among other soft skills

 

A Story of Transformation

Once applicants complete the SAP Young Professionals, they get two Certifications as SAP Associate Consultants; validating that they are equipped to use, build and maintain the intelligent platforms of tomorrow, while also ensuring AI becomes a catalyst for efficiency.

I recently had the privilege of discussing the future of the ecosystem with a standout YPP alumni who embodies this shift and he is the answer to that 2/10 readiness barrier. In his own words, he shares how the YPP allowed him to merge technical S/4HANA expertise with the critical thinking required to navigate a digital transformation economy:

 

 

Cultivating the Next Era

The goal for the SAP ecosystem is clear: we must ensure our teams are prepared for rapid technological acceleration. By integrating SAP certified talent, organizations inject Cloud and AI-native stability into their digital roadmaps.

The future of Intelligent Enterprise is already here, and the secret to success is empowering the people ready to run it.

If you want to check out the pool of SAP Associate Consultants, please reach out to digitalskillscenter@sap.comç

____________________________________________________________________________________________________

I'd like to read you 👀 

What’s your strategy for overcoming the skills gap? Whether you are a partner, customer or SAP-related, let’s discuss how fresh, YPP-certified perspectives are changing the game in your region.

 

1. World Economic Forum. (2025). New Economy Skills: Building AI, Data and Digital Capabilities for Growth. *WEF_New_Economy_Skills_2025.pdf

2. World Economic Forum. (2025). Future of Jobs Report. WEF_Future_of_Jobs_Report_2025.pdf
